title: The Autobiography of an Idea
description: The Autobiography of an Idea — knowledge fragments for AI agents

memes.label: About This Wiki
memes.description: A knowledge wiki for "The Autobiography of an Idea" — this page is auto-generated from book metadata

memes.label: 'Table of Contents'
memes.description: 'Table of Contents'

memes.label: Adler & Sullivan
memes.description: The architectural firm co-founded by Louis Sullivan and Dankmar Adler in 1881, which produced the Auditorium Building, Wainwright Building, and other landmark works of the Chicago School

memes.label: Adler and Sullivan Partnership (1881)
memes.description: The founding of Adler & Sullivan on May 1, 1881, the partnership that launched Sullivan's career and produced landmarks of the Chicago School

memes.label: Andrienne List Sullivan
memes.description: Mother of Louis H. Sullivan, born in Geneva, Switzerland; a pianist of remarkable talent whose musical sensibility and emotional character profoundly influenced her son's early development.

memes.label: architectural-concepts
memes.description: Cross-type index of architectural theory and practice concepts in Sullivan's thought — from skyscraper design to organic ornament, from structural engineering to aesthetic principle.

memes.label: Architectural Progress
memes.description: Sullivan's conviction that architecture, like nature, must evolve — progressing from simple forms to complex organisms through continuous growth, not by imitating the past

memes.label: Architectural Tradition
memes.description: Sullivan's critical view of architectural tradition as a "velvet and down" that smothers creative originality — the dead hand of precedent against which modern architecture must rebel

memes.label: Architectural Truth
memes.description: Sullivan's principle that architecture must honestly express its materials, structure, and purpose — truth as the foundation of all genuine beauty in building

memes.label: Artistic Inspiration
memes.description: Sullivan's theory that true architectural inspiration comes from direct communion with nature rather than from books, precedents, or academic training

memes.label: Atelier Vaudremer
memes.description: The Paris atelier of architect Émile Vaudremer on the Rue de Bac where Louis Sullivan trained in 1874–1875, immersing himself in the competitive, collegial culture of the Beaux-Arts system while simultaneously beginning to question its doctrines

memes.label: Auditorium Building
memes.description: Multi-use building complex in Chicago (1889) combining a 4,250-seat theater, hotel, and office tower — Sullivan and Adler's masterpiece of the masonry period, the project that established their firm alongside Burnham & Root as leaders of the Chicago School

memes.label: Beauty
memes.description: Louis Sullivan's conception of Beauty as an intrinsic quality of Nature and a necessary attribute of authentic architecture.

memes.label: Beauty in Architecture
memes.description: Sullivan's conception of beauty as nature's manifestation in built form — an organic quality arising from truthful expression of purpose rather than applied decoration

memes.label: Beneficent Power
memes.description: Sullivan's conviction that creative power — imagination, will, and architectural vision — must be directed toward beneficent ends, serving humanity rather than oppressing it, forming the moral foundation of his architectural philosophy

memes.label: Berkshire Hills Revelation
memes.description: The pivotal childhood moment when young Louis Sullivan, traveling by train through the Berkshire Hills at dawn, experienced an overwhelming revelation of nature's sublimity and the power of man — an awakening that seeded his lifelong architectural philosophy

memes.label: Borden Block
memes.description: Six-story high-grade office building in Chicago, one of Louis Sullivan's first major independent design opportunities as head of Adler's office in 1879

memes.label: Boston
memes.description: Sullivan's birthplace and early childhood city, where he encountered his first formal education and experienced the constraining atmosphere that drove him to seek wider horizons

memes.label: Building Character
memes.description: Sullivan's principle that every building must possess individual character — a personality arising from its unique purpose, site, materials, and the creative intelligence shaping it

memes.label: Burnham & Root
memes.description: The rival Chicago architectural firm of Daniel Burnham and John Wellborn Root, representing establishment tradition against which Sullivan defined his modernism, and the firm that directed the 1893 World's Columbian Exposition

memes.label: "I: The Child"
memes.description: "The early childhood of Louis Sullivan in South Reading and Boston, and the story of his parents"

memes.label: "II"
memes.description: "Sullivan's boyhood years, his growing awareness of the world around him"

memes.label: "III: And Then Came Spring!"
memes.description: "Spring arrives and with it new experiences and awakenings in young Sullivan's life"

memes.label: "IV: A Vacation"
memes.description: "A formative vacation that left lasting impressions on the young architect-to-be"

memes.label: "V: Newburyport"
memes.description: "Sullivan's time in Newburyport, where his architectural sensibilities began to take shape"

memes.label: "VI: Boston"
memes.description: "Early experiences in Boston that shaped Sullivan's architectural vision"

memes.label: "VII: Boston"
memes.description: "Continued accounts of Sullivan's formative years in Boston"

memes.label: "VIII: Louis Goeth on a Journey"
memes.description: "Louis embarks on a journey that would broaden his horizons and influence his future work"

memes.label: "IX: Boston—The English High School"
memes.description: "Sullivan's education at the English High School in Boston"

memes.label: "X: Farewell to Boston"
memes.description: "Sullivan bids farewell to Boston and prepares for new adventures westward"

memes.label: "XI: Chicago"
memes.description: "Arrival in Chicago, the city that would become the canvas for Sullivan's greatest works"

memes.label: "XII: Paris"
memes.description: "Sullivan's time in Paris, studying at the École des Beaux-Arts and absorbing European architectural traditions"

memes.label: "XIII: The Garden City"
memes.description: "Sullivan reflects on the garden city movement and its implications for American architecture"

memes.label: "XIV: Face to Face"
memes.description: "A pivotal confrontation — Sullivan face to face with the architectural establishment"

memes.label: "XV: Retrospect"
memes.description: "Sullivan looks back on his life, philosophy, and the idea that animated his work"

memes.label: Charles Darwin
memes.description: Charles Darwin (1809–1882), English naturalist whose theory of evolution by natural selection, particularly in *On the Origin of Species*, shaped Louis Sullivan's view of organic development in nature and architecture

memes.label: Chicago
memes.description: The city where Louis Sullivan built his professional career and became the center of American architectural innovation in the 1880s-1890s

memes.label: chicago-nexus
memes.description: A cross-type aggregation of all Chicago-connected entities — the city that shaped Sullivan's career, from its architecture firms and buildings to the 1893 World's Fair and the Chicago School.

memes.label: The Chicago School: A Collaborative Portrait
memes.description: A narrative synthesis examining how the post-Fire rebuilding of Chicago, an unusually open partnership culture among architects and engineers, and Sullivan's experimental method combined to create the conditions for a distinctly American modern architecture

memes.label: Childhood
memes.description: Louis Sullivan's conception of childhood as the primordial source of creative instinct, imagination, and architectural vision.

memes.label: Concept Index
memes.description: Complete index of all concept entries — aesthetic, philosophical, and architectural ideas explored in The Autobiography of an Idea.

memes.label: Creative Will
memes.description: Sullivan's conception of will as the executive force that translates imaginative vision into reality through persistent effort, discipline, and the dogged determination to carry a creative program through to completion

memes.label: Daniel Burnham
memes.description: Daniel Hudson Burnham (1846–1912), architect and urban planner, partner at Burnham & Root, Chief of Construction for the 1893 World's Columbian Exposition; Sullivan's rival and friend, "obsessed by the feudal idea of power" (015-005), representing the commercial, organizational approach to architecture that Sullivan rejected

memes.label: Dankmar Adler
memes.description: Dankmar Adler (1844–1900), architect and acoustical engineer, Sullivan's partner at Adler & Sullivan (1880–1895), a "technician, an engineer, a conscientious administrator" with a "large progressive judicial and judicious mind" who provided the practical foundation for Sullivan's creative work

memes.label: Democracy (Sullivan)
memes.description: Sullivan's foundational political and architectural conviction — that democracy is the beneficent, life-affirming alternative to feudal power, and the only legitimate basis for an authentic American architecture. First articulated in contrast to Daniel Burnham's feudal ambition (015-005), developed into a full critique of the World's Columbian Exposition (015-086–089).

memes.label: democracy-and-creative-freedom
memes.description: Pages exploring Sullivan's democratic idealism — the conviction that architecture must express the freedom, dignity, and creative spirit of a democratic society.

memes.label: Democracy in Architecture
memes.description: Sullivan's vision that true architecture must embody democratic ideals -- serving all people, not elites -- and that American architecture's destiny is to express the moral and spiritual power of democracy

memes.label: Descriptive Geometry
memes.description: The mathematical discipline of representing three-dimensional objects in two dimensions, which Sullivan studied intensively under Monsieur Clopet in Paris — a technical foundation he later transcended in favor of a more organic architectural philosophy, yet whose rigorous method of demonstration left a permanent imprint on his thinking

memes.label: Dream
memes.description: Louis Sullivan's conception of the Dream as a mode of creative vision — the capacity to see beyond the actual toward what might be, and to hold that vision with sufficient conviction that it could eventually be built.

memes.label: Ecole des Beaux-Arts
memes.description: The French national school of fine arts in Paris where Sullivan studied architecture in 1874, whose rigorous intellectual training profoundly shaped his systematic thinking even as he ultimately rejected its premises

memes.label: Education
memes.description: Sullivan's evolving views on formal and informal education as explored in his autobiography.

memes.label: Education of the Architect
memes.description: Sullivan's lifelong critique of formal architectural schooling and his alternative philosophy — that architects must be educated through direct experience of nature, self-discipline, and the cultivation of imagination, not through the rigid "Orders," artificial classroom routines, or the imitation of historical styles

memes.label: Émile Vaudremer
memes.description: French architect (1829–1914), winner of the Grand Prix de Rome, who ran the atelier libre in Paris where Louis Sullivan studied from 1874 to 1875 and whom Sullivan deeply respected as "Le Patron."

memes.label: Emotional Expression in Architecture
memes.description: Sullivan's conviction that architecture must convey genuine human emotion — that buildings, like their creators, must express the full range of feeling from primal joy to spiritual contemplation

memes.label: English High School (Boston)
memes.description: The Boston public high school Louis Sullivan entered at age fourteen in 1870, where he encountered the master teacher Moses Woolson — a formative experience Sullivan credited throughout his life as the foundation of his intellectual discipline and independence

memes.label: Event Index
memes.description: Chronological index of key events in The Autobiography of an Idea — from the Great Chicago Fire of 1871 to Sullivan's professional milestones and the World's Columbian Exposition of 1893.

memes.label: Featured Pages
memes.description: Curated collection of featured and premium quality pages — the highest-quality entries in The Autobiography of an Idea wiki.

memes.label: Feudal Thought
memes.description: Sullivan's critique of the persistent medieval mindset of hierarchy, fear, and domination that he identified as the antithesis of democratic modernity — a mentality that, he argued, still held architecture and civilization in its grip

memes.label: Form Follows Function
memes.description: Sullivan's foundational architectural principle that the shape of a building should be primarily determined by its intended purpose, derived from his observation of living organisms in nature

memes.label: France
memes.description: The cultural and intellectual homeland of the French influence in Louis Sullivan's life — his mother's language, his father's dance training, and the architectural tradition of the École des Beaux-Arts that shaped his formal education.

memes.label: Frank Furness
memes.description: Philadelphia architect (1839–1912), partner in Furness & Hewitt, who gave Louis Sullivan his first architectural job in 1873 and profoundly influenced his conception of the architect as a creative force.

memes.label: Freedom
memes.description: Sullivan's conception of freedom as a personal, intellectual, and creative necessity.

memes.label: Freedom of Thought
memes.description: Sullivan's conviction that intellectual and creative freedom is the prerequisite for genuine art and architecture — that traditions, conventions, and received authority suppress the creative spirit and must be cast aside for authentic expression

memes.label: Geneva
memes.description: The Swiss city where Louis Sullivan's maternal grandmother, Anna Mattheus List, was born, and where the List family prospered before financial ruin drove them to emigrate to America.

memes.label: Great Chicago Fire (1871)
memes.description: The fire of October 8-10, 1871 that destroyed much of Chicago — which Sullivan experienced as a distant witness and later interpreted as revealing the spirit of man rising from the ashes to rebuild, creating the conditions for the birth of the Chicago School and the modern skyscraper

memes.label: Henri List
memes.description: Henri List, Louis Sullivan's maternal grandfather — a German immigrant of mysterious past, educated for the Catholic priesthood before rebelling, later a professor of Greek at Geneva, whose life was marked by financial collapses, cupidity, and a "cynic hand" that appeared even in his wife's death notice

memes.label: Henry Hobson Richardson
memes.description: Henry Hobson Richardson (1838–1886), the leading American architect Sullivan admired — designer of Trinity Church in Boston and the Marshall Field Wholesale Store in Chicago, whom Sullivan called "he of the strong arm and virile mind — sole giant of his day"

memes.label: Herbert Spencer
memes.description: Herbert Spencer (1820–1903), English philosopher whose evolutionary theory — defining development as progression from simple to complex — deeply influenced Louis Sullivan's organic thinking about architecture and the growth of ideas

memes.label: Hippolyte Taine
memes.description: French philosopher, historian, and critic (1828–1893) whose theory that art is a direct expression of the life of a people profoundly shaped Louis Sullivan's architectural philosophy.

memes.label: Hudson River
memes.description: The great river of eastern New York whose scenic valley — framed by the Berkshire Hills and Catskill Mountains — made a profound impression on the young Louis Sullivan during a childhood journey, awakening his sensitivity to landscape grandeur.

memes.label: Imagination
memes.description: Sullivan's conception of imagination as a creative force central to artistic and architectural development.

memes.label: Inspiration
memes.description: A central aesthetic and philosophical concept in Louis Sullivan's thought, encompassing creative impulse, artistic vision, and the spiritual source of authentic architectural expression.

memes.label: Instinctive Creativity
memes.description: Sullivan's belief that the deepest creative power resides in instinct rather than intellect — the child's primal connection to nature as the true source of artistic genius

memes.label: Ireland
memes.description: The ancestral homeland of Patrick Sullivan, Louis Sullivan's father, whose stories of Irish county fairs, tribal warfare, and natural beauty shaped the young Sullivan's imaginative world.

memes.label: John Draper
memes.description: American scientist and historian (1811--1882) whose *Intellectual Development of Europe* (1862), a Christmas gift from Sullivan's father, gave Sullivan a panoramic framework of the "Age of Faith" and "Age of Reason" that reshaped his architectural thinking

memes.label: John Edelmann
memes.description: John Edelmann, Sullivan's closest intellectual friend in his formative years, originator of the theory of "suppressed functions" that became foundational to Sullivan's architectural philosophy; "the benefactor and Louis the parasite and profiteer" (011-032)

memes.label: John Wellborn Root
memes.description: John Wellborn Root (1850–1891), architect and partner at Burnham & Root; a magnetic, brilliant, and vain personality who "had it in him to be great" (015-011); designer of the Masonic Temple, Chicago's first true skyscraper; died at forty-one, leaving "a void in his wake" (015-011)

memes.label: Louis Sullivan
memes.description: Louis Henri Sullivan (1856–1924), "father of the skyscraper" and author of the seminal dictum "form follows function," pioneer of the Chicago School of architecture, partner in Adler & Sullivan, and the author of this autobiography — a radical thinker who transformed American architecture by insisting that buildings must express their inner purpose organically

memes.label: Man the Spirit
memes.description: Sullivan's conviction that humanity is fundamentally a spiritual being — that "Man" possesses innate creative powers which make him a "free spirit" and "creator," whose history is the unfolding of that spirit seeking expression through civilization

memes.label: Masonic Temple (Chicago)
memes.description: Landmark Chicago skyscraper designed by John Wellborn Root of Burnham & Root — at the time of its completion, the tallest building in the world

memes.label: Massachusetts Institute of Technology
memes.description: The engineering and technical school in Boston where Sullivan briefly studied architecture before leaving in disillusionment for Philadelphia and ultimately Paris

memes.label: Michelangelo
memes.description: Renaissance painter and sculptor whose Sistine Chapel ceiling became a pivotal test of Sullivan's visual judgment — Sullivan traveled to Rome specifically to verify whether he could detect the "momentum" Taine claimed was obvious in the Last Judgment

memes.label: Minnie
memes.description: A spirited eighteen-year-old young woman who befriended fourteen-year-old Louis Sullivan during his stay in Newburyport, becoming his "precious teacher" of worldly wisdom, French, and social understanding.

memes.label: Monadnock Building
memes.description: Pioneering Chicago skyscraper designed by Burnham & Root, noted by Sullivan as a "big jump" in the evolution of the tall office building — a transitional work that pushed load-bearing masonry to its structural limit.

memes.label: Monsieur Clopet
memes.description: Sullivan's mathematics tutor at the Ecole des Beaux-Arts in Paris, whose rigorous method of demonstration -- "such as to admit of no exception" -- became a lifelong model for Sullivan's approach to architectural problem-solving

memes.label: Montauk Block
memes.description: Large nine-story office building in Chicago by Burnham & Root — a milestone in the development of the tall commercial building

memes.label: Moses Woolson
memes.description: Moses Woolson, Sullivan's master at Boston's English High School, a "blend of wild man and of poet" (009-032) whose "genius as a teacher" (009-039) transformed the young Sullivan into a "compacted personality" (009-040); the single teacher for whom Sullivan felt lifelong gratitude

memes.label: Most Referenced Pages
memes.description: Top 50 pages ranked by total cross-references across The Autobiography of an Idea wiki, reflecting the most interconnected topics in Sullivan's thought.

memes.label: Nature
memes.description: Louis Sullivan's conception of Nature as the supreme teacher, source of beauty, and foundational principle for architecture.

memes.label: nature-and-human-spirit
memes.description: Thematic aggregation of pages exploring Sullivan's nature philosophy — the belief that organic growth, natural forms, and the human spirit are the true sources of architectural creativity.

memes.label: Nature as Model
memes.description: Sullivan's conviction that architecture should emulate nature's organic processes — where form develops from within according to function — rather than copy historical styles, drawing on Darwinian evolution, Spencerian growth, and direct observation of living organisms

memes.label: New York
memes.description: The Empire State's great metropolis — a stopover city in Sullivan's early journey, a foil to Chicago's organic growth, and an exemplar of what Sullivan diagnosed as the "crassness" and "provincialism" of eastern American architecture.

memes.label: Newburyport
memes.description: The coastal Massachusetts town where Sullivan spent idyllic childhood summers with his maternal grandparents, a formative landscape of farm, sea, and freedom that shaped his early sense of wonder

memes.label: Ocean Springs
memes.description: The Mississippi Gulf Coast village where Sullivan recovered from nervous exhaustion in 1890 and found a peaceful retreat where he wrote much of his autobiography

memes.label: Organic Growth
memes.description: Sullivan's adoption of Herbert Spencer's evolutionary theory — that all development proceeds from a simple unorganized state through stages of growth and differentiation toward a highly organized complex — applied to his own intellectual formation, to architecture, and to civilization

memes.label: Organization Index
memes.description: Alphabetical index of all organizations, institutions, firms, and schools in The Autobiography of an Idea — architectural firms, educational institutions, and professional bodies that shaped Sullivan's world.

memes.label: Panic of 1873
memes.description: The severe economic depression beginning September 1873 that shaped Chicago's economy during Sullivan's arrival and early architectural career

memes.label: Paris
memes.description: The city where Sullivan studied at the Ecole des Beaux-Arts in 1874, a transformative year that shaped his architectural vision and gave him confidence in his own creative path

memes.label: Patrick Sullivan
memes.description: Sullivan's father, an Irish dancing master of "grim will" who emigrated to America, established a successful academy in Boston, and imposed a regime of physical discipline and self-reliance on his young son

memes.label: Person Index
memes.description: Complete index of all person entries in The Autobiography of an Idea wiki, sorted alphabetically with quality ratings and reference counts.

memes.label: Philadelphia
memes.description: City where Sullivan began his formal architectural training in the office of Furness & Hewitt, and where he encountered the Centennial Exposition of 1876 — an event that set the course of his career.

memes.label: philosophy-themes
memes.description: Cross-type aggregation of pages tagged with core philosophical themes in Sullivan's autobiography — exploring idealism, pragmatism, and the philosophical foundations of organic architecture.

memes.label: Place Index
memes.description: Complete index of all place entries — cities, buildings, and landscapes that shaped Louis Sullivan's life and architectural career.

memes.label: Plastic Architecture
memes.description: Sullivan's doctrine that architecture must be "plastic" — malleable, responsive to function, and organic in its unfolding — rather than rigidly bound by conventions; the direct architectural application of his organic philosophy of growth

memes.label: Portland Block
memes.description: Four-story Chicago commercial building that Sullivan studied early in his career, notable for the incidental discovery that it had been designed not by its reputed architect but by another hand — an early lesson in the opacity of architectural authorship.

memes.label: Power of Imagination
memes.description: Sullivan's conviction that imagination is the fundamental creative faculty — the power to envision what does not yet exist, and through effort of will, to bring it into reality

memes.label: Most Quoted Pages
memes.description: Pages ranked by number of direct quotations from The Autobiography of an Idea — a guide to the wiki entries richest in Sullivan's own words and primary-source evidence.

memes.label: Ralph Waldo Emerson
memes.description: Ralph Waldo Emerson (1803–1882), American essayist, poet, and transcendentalist philosopher whose call for "an original relation to the universe" deeply influenced Sullivan's democratic philosophy of creative individualism.

memes.label: Reason and Intuition
memes.description: Sullivan's synthesis of rational analysis with intuitive perception — the belief that great architecture requires both the discipline of reasoned method and the illumination of childlike intuition

memes.label: Rhythm
memes.description: Sullivan's understanding of rhythm as a fundamental ordering principle in nature, art, and architecture.

memes.label: Rhythm of Life
memes.description: 'Sullivan''s lyrical conviction that life, nature, and architecture all participate in a universal rhythm — a temporal, aesthetic, and organic principle by which all "living forms" pulse, move, and develop in alternating patterns of growth and decay, action and stillness, much like the turning of the seasons or the phrasing of music'

memes.label: Richard Wagner
memes.description: Richard Wagner (1813–1883), German composer whose music profoundly moved Louis Sullivan, representing to him the power of artistic creation and the free spirit that creates from within

memes.label: Romance
memes.description: Louis Sullivan's conception of Romance as an essential quality of experience — the spirit of adventure, wonder, and creative vitality that must animate architecture.

memes.label: Rome
memes.description: The Eternal City, visited by Sullivan at age eighteen in a pilgrimage to the Sistine Chapel, where Michelangelo's ceiling revealed to him the spiritual law that would shape his theory of creative will and organic architecture.

memes.label: Scientific Method in Architecture
memes.description: Sullivan's application of the scientific method — observation, hypothesis, experiment, and verification — to architectural practice, drawn from his study of 19th-century science (Draper, Darwin, Spencer, Huxley, Tyndall) and Clopet's mathematical rigor, making architecture a discipline of systematic inquiry rather than received tradition

memes.label: South Reading
memes.description: A village in New England, ten miles north of Boston — the site of Louis Sullivan's grandparents' farm where he spent formative childhood years from age five, later explored as the wilderness of memory and the origin of his sensitivity to nature's ordered beauty

memes.label: St. Louis
memes.description: Major Mississippi River city whose rapid growth and the Wainwright Building commission placed it at the center of Sullivan's early skyscraper practice, and whose rivalry with Chicago defined the urban ambitions of the late nineteenth-century Midwest.

memes.label: Steel-Frame Construction
memes.description: The revolutionary structural system — a steel skeleton carrying all loads independently of exterior walls — that liberated tall buildings from the physical and economic constraints of masonry construction, enabling the modern skyscraper

memes.label: Stoneham
memes.description: Massachusetts town adjacent to South Reading, connected by the Stoneham Road that served as a primary artery through Sullivan's childhood landscape and a recurring setting in his autobiographical recollections of rural New England life.

memes.label: Sublimity (Sullivan)
memes.description: Sullivan's aesthetic concept of sublimity — the overwhelming experience of nature's grandeur and the creative power of man, encountered first in the Berkshire Hills and developed throughout his autobiography as the spiritual foundation of architectural aspiration

memes.label: Sullivan's Grandparents' Farm
memes.description: The twenty-four-acre farm in South Reading/Wakefield, Massachusetts, where Sullivan spent formative childhood years absorbing the nature that became the foundation of his architectural philosophy

memes.label: Suppressed Functions
memes.description: John Edelmann's theory that living organisms — and by extension architecture and societies — possess latent functional capacities that are systematically suppressed by convention, tradition, and inadequate conditions; a pivotal intellectual breakthrough that "lit up" Sullivan's inner and outer world and helped him see that architecture "must not suppress"

memes.label: Sympathy with Nature
memes.description: Sullivan's belief that the architect must cultivate a deep sympathetic bond with nature — not as a source of motifs to copy but as a teacher of organic principles and creative power

memes.label: Topics by Tag
memes.description: Browse wiki entries by thematic tag — philosophy, architectural theory, creativity, nature, democracy, education, and the other conceptual threads that weave through The Autobiography of an Idea.

memes.label: The Tall Office Building
memes.description: The defining architectural problem of late 19th-century Chicago — the tall office building arose from economic pressure on land values, was made possible by the passenger elevator and the steel frame, and demanded an entirely new architectural language that Sullivan supplied through his principle of form following function

memes.label: The Idea
memes.description: Sullivan's self-referential concept of "the Idea" — the central theme of his autobiography's title, describing his own life as the unfolding of a single architectural philosophy that grew organically from childhood through maturity

memes.label: The Power of Man
memes.description: Sullivan's foundational belief that humanity possesses an innate, enormous, and ever-growing power to create, build, and shape its own destiny — a childhood intuition that grew into the philosophical cornerstone of his architectural and democratic thought

memes.label: Transportation Building
memes.description: Sullivan's sole contribution to the 1893 World's Columbian Exposition — a polychromatic, unhistorical pavilion whose celebrated Golden Doorway stood as the lone gesture of architectural modernism amid the White City's neoclassical uniformity.

memes.label: Truth
memes.description: Sullivan's philosophical commitment to truth as the foundation of authentic architecture and honest living.

memes.label: Wainwright Building
memes.description: Nine-story office building in St. Louis (1891), designed by Louis Sullivan of Adler & Sullivan — the first building to give authentic architectural expression to steel-frame construction in its exterior treatment, embodying Sullivan's conviction that the new engineering "demanded an equally revolutionary architectural mode" (015-027)

memes.label: Walt Whitman
memes.description: Walt Whitman (1819–1892), American poet whose democratic vision, celebration of nature, and expansive free verse in *Leaves of Grass* resonated with Louis Sullivan's own philosophy of organic growth and the creative power of the individual spirit

memes.label: William Le Baron Jenney
memes.description: Chicago architect and engineer (1832--1907), pioneer of steel-frame construction, Sullivan's first architectural employer in Chicago, and a free-and-easy cultured gentleman whose office was a breeding ground for the Chicago School

memes.label: Work Index
memes.description: Index of built works and architectural projects in The Autobiography of an Idea — the skyscrapers, office blocks, and landmark buildings of Sullivan's Chicago and beyond.

memes.label: World's Columbian Exposition (1893)
memes.description: The 1893 Chicago World's Fair — the "White City" of neoclassical exhibition halls directed by Daniel Burnham — where Louis Sullivan's Transportation Building stood as the sole gesture of architectural modernism amid a sea of imported classicism. Sullivan regarded the Fair as a catastrophic betrayal of democratic architecture whose "virus" (015-086) would infect American building for half a century.
